statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
We call an array of numbers $ k $ -exquisite if it has at least two elements and any two adjacent numbers differ by at least $ k $ .

You are given a permutation $ ^{\text{∗}} $ $ p $ of length $ n $ . For each $ k $ from $ 1 $ to $ n - 1 $ , find the number of $ k $ -exquisite subarrays $ ^{\text{†}} $ .

$ ^{\text{∗}} $ A permutation of length $ n $ is an array that contains each integer from $ 1 $ to $ n $ exactly once, in any order.

$ ^{\text{†}} $ A subarray of an array is a sequence of one or more consecutive elements of the array.

Input Format
Each test consists of several test cases. The first line contains a single integer $ t $ $ (1 \le t \le 25000) $ — the number of test cases. The descriptions of the test cases follow.

In the first line of each test case, an integer $ n $ is given — the length of the permutation $ (2 \le n \le 10^5) $ .

In the second line of each test case, $ n $ integers $ p_i $ are given — the elements of the permutation $ (1 \le p_i \le n) $ . It is guaranteed that $ p_i $ are distinct.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 2 \cdot 10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output the number of $ k $ -exquisite subarrays for all $ k $ from $ 1 $ to $ n - 1 $ .

Examples
Sample 1
Input
3
5
5 1 4 2 3
3
3 2 1
4
3 1 2 4
Output
10 6 3 1
3 0
6 2 0

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: If some array is $$$k$$$-exquisite, then any of its subsegments of length at least $$$2$$$ is also $$$k$$$-exquisite.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: If $$$k_1 \gt k_2$$$ and some array is $$$k_1$$$-exquisite, then it is also $$$k_2$$$-exquisite.

hints.title: Hint 3
hints.content:
If some array of length $$$m$$$ is $$$k$$$-exquisite, how many $$$k$$$-exquisite subsegments does it have?
Answer
$$$\frac{m \cdot (m - 1)}{2}$$$.

hints.title: Hint 4
hints.content: For a fixed $$$k$$$, come up with a way to divide the entire original permutation into segments, each of which is either $$$k$$$-exquisite or of length $$$1$$$, so that you can use the formula from the previous hint.

hints.title: Hint 5
hints.content:
How to transition from the segmentation for $$$k+1$$$ to the segmentation for $$$k$$$?
Answer
You need to find all positions where the difference between the elements of the permutation equals $$$k$$$, and merge the corresponding segments.

hints.title: Hint 6
hints.content: Determine how the answer changes with each merging of two segments.

solutions.title: Editorial
solutions.content:
2184E - Exquisite Array
It is not difficult to see that if $$$k_1  \gt  k_2$$$ and some subsegment is $$$k_1$$$-exquisite, then any of its subsegments of length at least $$$2$$$ is also $$$k_1$$$-exquisite, and they are $$$k_2$$$-exquisite as well. This means that we can iterate $$$k$$$ from $$$n-1$$$ to $$$1$$$ (since the absolute difference between adjacent elements of the permutation can only take values from the interval $$$[1; n-1]$$$).
For each possible value of $$$k$$$, we will remember all positions where the absolute difference between adjacent elements of the permutation equals $$$k$$$.
Initially, we will divide the entire permutation into segments consisting of a single element. We will iterate $$$k$$$ from $$$n-1$$$ to $$$1$$$. After each subsequent step with a fixed $$$k$$$, these segments will represent a partition into $$$k$$$-exquisite segments and segments of length $$$1$$$, such that there is no $$$k$$$-exquisite subsegment that is not entirely part of one of the segments in the partition. This can be achieved by maintaining the minimum absolute difference between adjacent elements of each segment to be at least $$$k$$$. For a fixed $$$k$$$, we will consider all positions where the absolute difference between adjacent elements of the permutation equals $$$k$$$, and we will merge the corresponding segments.
After we have performed all actions in this algorithm for a fixed $$$k$$$ and obtained the partition $$$segs$$$, the number of $$$k$$$-exquisite subsegments can be found using the formula $$$\sum_{seg \in segs} \frac{len(seg) \cdot (len(seg) - 1)}{2}$$$, where $$$len(seg)$$$ is the number of elements in the segment $$$seg$$$. Instead of recalculating this sum from scratch for each $$$k$$$, we will maintain this sum from previous steps of the algorithm, and when merging two segments, we will subtract the corresponding fractions of these two segments from the sum, and then add the corresponding fraction of the resulting segment.
Merging segments can be done using a DSU, or built-in data structures like set, or any other method you are familiar with. The overall complexity of the solution will be $$$O(n \cdot f(n))$$$, where $$$f(n)$$$ is the asymptotic complexity of merging two segments using the chosen method.
